content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN. An element XXX is said to be dominant if the frequency of XXX in AAA is strictly greater than the frequency of any other element in the AAA.

For example, if A=[2,1,4,4,4]A = [2, 1, 4, 4, 4]A=[2,1,4,4,4] then 444 is a dominant element since its frequency is higher than the frequency of any other element in AAA.

Find if there exists any dominant element in AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if there exists any dominant element in AAA. Otherwise, output `NO`.


You may print each character of `YES` and `NO` in uppercase or lowercase (for example, `yes`, `yEs`, `Yes` will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists a dominant element, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: element 2 appears 5 times, strictly more than any other → YES.
Test case 2: all frequencies are 1, no element is strictly dominant → NO.
Test case 3: element 3 appears twice, others once → YES.
Test case 4: frequencies 1 and 2 both appear twice, tie for max → NO.
The only value 2 occurs 3 times, which is strictly greater than any other (there are none).
Both 1 and 2 appear twice, so the maximum frequency is not unique.

solution.approach: For each test case count the frequency of every value using a hash map (or an array of size N+1 because Ai ≤ N). Find the maximum frequency maxFreq and how many distinct values attain this frequency (cntMax). If cntMax == 1 then a unique element has strictly higher frequency than all others, so answer is YES; otherwise answer is NO. This runs in O(N) time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the frequency container (at most N+1 integers).
